content.full_text:
Farn Por-rrrcal PnacrrcEs CourrarssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620. Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
January 10,2003
Rebecca J. Turrentine, Ed.D.
Bellflower Unified School District
16703 South Clark Avenue
Bellflower, CA90706
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 4-02-303
Dear Dr. Turrentine:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Bill Ste. Marie
regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1
QUESTTONS
1. May Mr. Ste. Marie participate in the discussion on the topic of taking back
the special education programs from the county?
2. May he vote on hiring a consultant to study the issue of the district taking
back its special education programs from the county?
3. May he vote on the decision to take back programs?
CONCLUSION
1,2, & 3: Holding a position as a special education teacher employed by the Los
Angeles County Office of Education while simultaneously working as a board member
with the Bellflower Unified School District Board of Education does not in itself create a
conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act. Due to the governmental salary
exception, a public official does not have a conflict of interest in a decision involving his
or her employer, if the employer is a govemmental agency. The Los Angeles County
Office of Education is a goverrimental agency, as is the Bellflower Unified School
District Board of Education. Therefore, Mr. Ste. Marie's income does not result in a
conflict of interest in decisions pertaining to either the school district or the board of
education, unless he is personally affected by a decision. It would be advisable to contact
' Governmert Code sections 81000 - gl0l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
1 8 109- 18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.


File No. A-02-303
Page No. 2
the Attorney General's office concerning the doctrine of "incompatible offices" and other
provisions of law that might apply to your facts. Our advice is limited to the Act.
FACTS
A member of the Bellflower Unified School District Board of Education, Bill Ste.
Marie, is a special education teacher who is employed by the Los Angeles County Office
of Education. The County Office of Education provides many of the special education
services used by your district. Mr. Ste. Marie's classroom is located on the Bellflower
High School site which is one of the schools governed by the Bellflower Unified School
District Board of Education.
In June, you as a district, notified the Los Angeles County Office of Education
that you may potentially take back the responsibility for providing the special education
services for your district. As you stated, many of these services are currently provided by
the Los Angeles County Office of Education.
The issue of Mr. Ste. Marie's employment raises concern. He is a Los Angeles
County Office of Education employee, however, his job location is on one of the
Bellflower Unified School District's campuses. If the Board of Education decides to take
back your special education programs from the county, the result may be that Mr. Ste.
Marie could no longer work at Bellflower High School. He could not continue providing
the special education services as an employee on that site because he could not be a
member of the board of education and an employee of the district at the same time.
ANALYSIS
The purpose of the conflict-of-interest provisions in the Act is to ensure that
public officials use their authority in an impartial, unbiased manner. (Section 81001 (b).)
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from makin g, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which they know or have reason to know they have a disqualifting conflict of interest.
Under $ 87103, a public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning
of $ 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financiaf
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, or on a
member of his or her immediate family. The Commission has adopted a standard eight-
step analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifizing conflict of interest in
a given governmental decision. (Reg. 18700, subdivisions (b)(1)-(8).)
The first step in the analysis is to determine if Mr. Ste. Marie is a public official.
The Act's conflict-of-interest disqualifying provisions apply only to "public officials.,,
($$ 87100, 87103; reg. 18700, subd. (b)(1).) As a special education teacher employed by
the Los Angeles County Office of Education and as a board member of Bellflower High
School, Bill Ste. Marie qualifies as a public official in both capacities, and is subject to
the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. (Section 82048.)


File No. A-02-303
Page No. 3
The second step is to determine if the public official will be making, participating
in making or influencing a govemmental decision. A public official "makes a
governmental decision" when the official, actingwithin the authority of his or her office
or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of
action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Section
87100; regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in making a governmental
decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant
substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or makes
recommendations to the decision maker regarding the governmental decision. (Section
87100; regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a decision before his or her own agency if, for the purpose of
influencing, the official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or
consultant of his or her agency. (Section 87100; regulation 157023.)
As a board member with the Bellflower Unified School District, Mr. Ste. Marie
will "make a goveflrmental decision" or "participate in making" or "influence" that
decision, if; 1) he engages in the discussion on the topic of taking back the special
education programs from the county; 2) he votes on hiring a consultant to study the issue
of the district taking back its special education programs, or 3) if votes on the decision to
take back programs from the county.
Steps 3 - 6 analyze whether an economic interest of a public official is affected
by the decision and whether there is a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
the official's economic interest. (Section 87100.) Section 87103 provides that apublic
official has a disqualiffing "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or
her immediate family, or on any of the official's economic interests, described as follows:
a
a
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment2 of $2,000 or more. (section sito:1a;; regulation
1 8703. 1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, offi.cer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management (Section 87103(d); regulation 1s703.1(b));
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which a public official
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b), regulation
18703.2);
2 An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by
a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's immediate family, or their agents own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediate family" is defined
at$ 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.


a
a
File No. A-02-303
Page No. 4
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(c), regulation 1 8703.3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts, if the gifts aggregate
to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e); regulation
18703.$;
A public official also has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family-- (this is the "personal financial
effects" rule. (Section 87103; regulation 18703.5.)
Both the Bellflower School District and the Los Angeles County Board of
Education are govefilmental agencies. (Section 8204L.) Salary from a state, local or
federal govefirmental agency is expressly exempted from the definition of "income" for
purposes of the Act. (Section 82030(b)(2).) Thus, the salary Bill Ste. Marie receives is
not disqualifying "income" for purposes of section 87103(c). However, section 871,03
also states that the official has a financial intErest in a decision if the decision has a
material financial effect on the official or a member of his or her family. Under this
"personal effects" rule, a conflict of interest exists where a decision will result in a public
official's personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, or those of his or her immediate
family incieasing or decreasing by S250, regardless of the source of the increase or the
decrease. (Regulation 1 8703.5; regulations 187 04.5; 1 8705.5.)
For purposes of analyzing personal financial effects, we have stated that the
personal effect test will not serve to require an official to disqualifu himself or herself, if
the decision affects only the salary, per diem, or reimbursement for expenses the official
receives from a local governmental agency. (Regulation 18705.) Therefore, a decision
affecting an official's govemmental salary will not generally be a problem under this
rule, 'trnless the decision is to hire, fire, promote, demote, suspend without pay or
otherwise take disciplinary action with financial sanction against the official or a member
of his or her family, or to set a salary for the official or a member of his or her immediate
family which is different from salaries paid to other employees of the govemment agency
in the same job classification or position." (Regulation 18705.5(b).) The salary Mr. Ste.
Marie receives from both goveffrmental agencies is not a disqualifuing economic interest.
Under Mr. Ste. Marie's circumstances, a decision to abolish his position may not fit
under the exception because itmay affect more than his salary. Whether it is
substantially likely that Mr. Ste. Marie would lose his governmental position as a result,
is a question offact.
As provided in regulation 18703.5, Mr. Ste Marie may participate in district
decisions unless there will be a personal frnancial effect on the official as indicated
above.
Steps 7 and 8 pertain to two exceptions. We have no facts to determine if the
exceptions would apply.


File No. A-02-303
Page No. 5
As discussed earlier, our advice is limited to the Act. You may wish to contact
the Attorney General's office concerning other provisions of law, such as the law
goveming incompatible public offices and Government Code Section 1090.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
anAg;,u-- ";__
By
Marie Fain
Legal Analyst, Legal Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. May Mr. Ste. Marie participate in the discussion on the topic of taking back
the special education programs from the county?
2. May he vote on hiring a consultant to study the issue of the district taking
back its special education programs from the county?
3. May he vote on the decision to take back programs?

CONCLUSION: 1,2, & 3: Holding a position as a special education teacher employed by the Los
Angeles County Office of Education while simultaneously working as a board member
with the Bellflower Unified School District Board of Education does not in itself create a
conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act. Due to the governmental salary
exception, a public official does not have a conflict of interest in a decision involving his
or her employer, if the employer is a govemmental agency. The Los Angeles County
Office of Education is a goverrimental agency, as is the Bellflower Unified School
District Board of Education. Therefore, Mr. Ste. Marie's income does not result in a
conflict of interest in decisions pertaining to either the school district or the board of
education, unless he is personally affected by a decision. It would be advisable to contact
the Attorney General's office concerning the doctrine of "incompatible offices" and other
provisions of law that might apply to your facts. Our advice is limited to the Act.
